content: How do I know that a session is running in TMUXZ
How do I configure the status bar

Tue: May 12, 2026 05:44 PDT — GSD-T v3.26.11 — CURRENT

You can detect a tmux session a few different ways:

- **Inside a shell**: check `$TMUX` — it's set to the socket path when you're in a tmux session, empty otherwise. Quick test: `[ -n "$TMUX" ] && echo "in tmux" || echo "not in tmux"`.
- **From outside**: `tmux ls` lists active sessions; exits non-zero with "no server running" if none exist.
- **Specific session**: `tmux has-session -t <name>` returns exit 0 if it exists.
- **Visual cue**: tmux's status bar at the bottom of the terminal (unless disabled).

For scripts, `$TMUX` is the canonical check.
How do I navigate to .tmux.conf

Tue: May 12, 2026 05:45 PDT — GSD-T v3.26.11 — CURRENT

tmux status bar config goes in `~/.tmux.conf`. Key options:

- **Enable/position**: `set -g status on` and `set -g status-position top` (or `bottom`, default).
- **Colors**: `set -g status-style "bg=black,fg=white"` — background/foreground for the whole bar.
- **Left side**: `set -g status-left "#S "` — shows session name. `#H` = hostname, `#W` = window name.
- **Right side**: `set -g status-right "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M"` — date/time. Supports `strftime` format.
- **Length**: `set -g status-left-length 40` / `status-right-length 80` — increase if content gets truncated.
- **Window list style**: `setw -g window-status-current-style "bg=blue,fg=white,bold"` — highlight active window.
- **Refresh rate**: `set -g status-interval 5` — seconds between redraws (default 15).

Reload after editing: `tmux source-file ~/.tmux.conf` (or `prefix + r` if bound).

Format variables: `#S` session, `#W` window, `#H` host, `#P` pane, `#(shell-cmd)` runs a command, `#{?cond,a,b}` conditional. Full list: `man tmux` → FORMATS section.
It doesn't exist

Tue: May 12, 2026 05:48 PDT — GSD-T v3.26.11 — CURRENT

`~/.tmux.conf` lives in your home directory (`/Users/david/.tmux.conf`). To work with it:

- **Check if it exists**: `ls -la ~/.tmux.conf`
- **Create/edit**: `nano ~/.tmux.conf` (or `vim`, `code ~/.tmux.conf`, etc.)
- **cd to its directory**: `cd ~` — it's just your home folder.
- **View it**: `cat ~/.tmux.conf`

If it doesn't exist yet, just open it in your editor — saving will create it. tmux will pick it up on next start, or reload in a running session with `tmux source-file ~/.tmux.conf`.
Yes, what are the most common config settings?

Tue: May 12, 2026 05:50 PDT — GSD-T v3.26.11 — CURRENT

I'll create `~/.tmux.conf` with a solid starter config covering the most common tweaks.
prefix + r?

Created `~/.tmux.conf`. The most useful settings, grouped:

- **General**: 256/truecolor terminal, larger scrollback (50k lines), mouse on, windows numbered from 1 (easier to reach), auto-renumber on close.
- **Prefix**: kept default `C-b`; comments show how to rebind to `C-a` (screen-style).
- **Reload**: `prefix + r` re-sources the config.
- **Splits**: `prefix + |` horizontal, `prefix + -` vertical (instead of `"` and `%`), new panes/windows open in current path.
- **Navigation**: `prefix + h/j/k/l` to move between panes (vim-style), `prefix + H/J/K/L` to resize (repeatable).
- **Copy mode**: vim keys (`v` to select, `y` to copy to macOS clipboard via `pbcopy`).
- **Status bar**: blue session-name chip on left, date/time on right, active window highlighted, activity monitoring on.

To apply now: start tmux (`tmux`) or reload in an existing session with `tmux source-file ~/.tmux.conf`. Edit any section to taste — reload again with `prefix + r`.
None of those key combination are working.

Tue: May 12, 2026 05:58 PDT — GSD-T v3.26.11 — CURRENT

Most likely cause: you're not actually inside a tmux session, so tmux key bindings have nothing to intercept. Let's check.
I just did 4.  Still the same.  I press Ctrl + A and then I release the keys and press R and I see an R in the command line prompt.

You ARE in tmux (session: `projects`, 3 windows, attached). So the prefix should work. A few things to check:

**1. The config has been loaded?** Your shell was already in tmux when we wrote the file — tmux only reads `~/.tmux.conf` at server startup. Reload it manually from the shell:

```
tmux source-file ~/.tmux.conf
```

After that, `Ctrl-b` then `r` should reload (and flash "Config reloaded" in the status bar).

**2. Are you pressing the keys correctly?**
- Press **Ctrl** and **b** together, **release both**, then press the next key alone.
- Not: Ctrl-b-r all held down. Not: Ctrl held through the second key.
- For `prefix + |`: Ctrl-b, release, then Shift-\ (the pipe character).

**3. Quick sanity test** — try the unmodified default binding `Ctrl-b ?` (question mark). That shows the full key list. If even that does nothing, your terminal is swallowing Ctrl-b before tmux sees it (some terminals/keyboard tools intercept it).

**4. If Ctrl-b is being intercepted**, rebind to Ctrl-a — edit `~/.tmux.conf`, uncomment these lines:
```
unbind C-b
set -g prefix C-a
bind C-a send-prefix
```
Then `tmux source-file ~/.tmux.conf`.

Try `tmux source-file ~/.tmux.conf` first and tell me what happens when you press `Ctrl-b` then `?`.

bind-key    -T root         M-MouseDown3Status        display-menu -T "#[align=centre]#{window_index}:#{window_name}" -t = -x W -y W "#{?#{>:#{session_windows},1},,-}Swap Left" l { swap-window -t :-1 } "#{?#{>:#{session_windows},1},,-}Swap Right" r { swap-window -t :+1 } "#{?pane_marked_set,,-}Swap Marked" s { swap-window } '' Kill X { kill-window } Respawn R { respawn-window -k } "#{?pane_marked,Unmark,Mark}" m { select-pane -m } Rename n { command-prompt -F -I "#W" { rename-window -t "#{window_id}" "%%" } } '' "New After" w { new-window -a } "New At End" W { new-window }
david~/projects %

Tue: May 12, 2026 06:03 PDT — GSD-T v3.26.11 — CURRENT

That symptom — `r` echoing into the shell — means tmux isn't intercepting the prefix at all. The config switch to `C-a` is in the file, but the **running tmux server** hasn't reloaded it yet. Config edits don't auto-apply.

Run this in your tmux pane:

```
tmux source-file ~/.tmux.conf
```

Then immediately try `Ctrl-a` then `r`. You should see "Config reloaded" flash in the status bar.

**If it still doesn't work after sourcing**, check what tmux actually has bound:

```
tmux show-options -g prefix
tmux list-keys | grep -E "( r |C-a)"
```

That tells us whether the prefix is `C-a` (config loaded) or still `C-b` (config rejected — probably a syntax error earlier in the file). Paste the output and I'll take it from there.

One other possibility: **Ctrl-a is bound at the shell level** (in zsh/bash, Ctrl-a moves to start of line — that's readline, not tmux). But tmux runs *above* the shell, so it should grab Ctrl-a first... *if* the config is loaded. Sourcing is the fix.
Can't you check all this?

iTerm2's profile keymap is **empty** (`"Keyboard Map" = { };`). So iTerm2 isn't grabbing Ctrl-A from its prefs at all. It should be passing Ctrl-A straight through to tmux.

This is strange. Let me check if there's an actual problem with how tmux is receiving keys, and run a real diagnostic.

david~/projects % cat -v

^A
^A
^C
david~/projects %

Tue: May 12, 2026 06:08 PDT — GSD-T v3.26.11 — CURRENT

That's the smoking gun. `cat -v` printed `^A` — meaning **iTerm2 sent Ctrl-A straight to the shell, and tmux did NOT intercept it**. The tmux server is configured correctly (we verified `prefix C-a` is bound), but the running tmux client isn't grabbing the key.

This is the classic symptom of: **the tmux session is attached to a client that started before the config was loaded, AND something is preventing the new binding from activating in the current client.**

Wait — actually there's a simpler explanation I want to rule out first. Are you running this from inside a Claude Code session that itself is running inside tmux? Let me check.
What do I lose if I use Iterm shortcuts?

**Found it.** The smoking-gun line:

```
3146     1 tmux -CC new -A -s projects -c /Users/david/projects
```

You're running tmux in **`-CC` (control mode)**, also called **tmux integration mode**. Note the client line: `(attached,focused,control-mode,...)`.

In control mode, tmux is NOT a normal terminal session. iTerm2 takes over rendering and key handling — each tmux window/pane becomes a *native iTerm2 window/tab*. **tmux key bindings (Ctrl-a, Ctrl-b, etc.) do not apply** because iTerm2 is acting as the tmux client, intercepting everything itself. That's why Ctrl-A goes straight to the shell.

This is why nothing you did worked — the config is fine, the binding is fine, but the prefix never has a chance to fire in control mode.

**Your options:**

1. **Use iTerm2's native window/tab/split shortcuts** (recommended in -CC mode). In control mode, iTerm2 commands automatically drive tmux:
   - Cmd-T → new tmux window
   - Cmd-D / Cmd-Shift-D → split pane
   - Cmd-W → close pane
   - Cmd-arrow → navigate panes
   - The status bar config still works (it's rendered by tmux), but key bindings don't.

2. **Drop out of control mode** if you want classic tmux keybindings. Detach (Cmd-Shift-D in iTerm2, or close the iTerm tab) and re-attach without `-CC`:
   ```
   tmux attach -t projects
   ```
   Then `Ctrl-a r` etc. will work normally. The trade-off: no native iTerm2 integration.

3. **Hybrid**: keep using `-CC` and don't worry about prefix bindings — use iTerm2 shortcuts for navigation. The status bar config from earlier still applies.

What do you want — keep `-CC` (and use Cmd-shortcuts), or switch to plain tmux (and get the `Ctrl-a` bindings)?
I just killed Tmux and Iterm and reloaded iterm.  Why is my tab name "davids-mbp.lan"

Tue: May 12, 2026 06:11 PDT — GSD-T v3.26.11 — CURRENT

Honestly, not much for most workflows. The key trade-offs:

**What you lose with iTerm `-CC` mode + Cmd-shortcuts:**

- **Portable muscle memory**: Cmd-keys are Mac-only and iTerm-specific. SSH into a Linux box and use tmux there → Ctrl-a/Ctrl-b is universal, Cmd-T does nothing.
- **Custom keybindings**: anything you'd configure in `~/.tmux.conf` under `bind ...` (vim-style pane nav, custom splits, swap-window, etc.) is unreachable. iTerm's shortcuts are fixed to its own command set.
- **Copy mode** (`prefix [` → scrollback with vim/emacs nav, search, regex copy): in `-CC` mode iTerm handles scrollback natively, so you use Cmd-F / mouse selection instead. Some power users miss vim-mode search.
- **Some tmux commands without a Cmd equivalent**: e.g. join-pane, break-pane, swap-window, layouts (`prefix Space`), mark-and-swap. You can still invoke them from the command line (`tmux command-prompt` or `tmux swap-window -t 2`), but no quick chord.
- **Nested tmux**: if you ever attach to a remote tmux *over SSH* from inside a `-CC` session, you'll really want a working prefix on the local side too. Not impossible to work around, but awkward.

**What you keep:**

- All tmux semantics: sessions, windows, panes, persistence across iTerm restarts, status bar, scripted layouts.
- Mouse support, scroll, resize.
- Per-window/pane native iTerm features: profile switching, transparency, find, image rendering.

**Recommendation for your situation**: stay in `-CC` for daily Mac work — the integration is genuinely nice. If you find yourself needing tmux-specific power features later, you can either:
- Detach and re-attach without `-CC` for that session, or
- Run a *second* nested tmux (`tmux -L inner new`) where prefix bindings work normally.

The status-bar config we wrote earlier still applies in `-CC` mode (tmux renders the status line either way), so that's not lost.
Please set it in ~/.zshrc - Just show the folder name.  Then Reload it.

Good news: the title-setting code is **already in your `~/.zshrc`** (lines 53–60). It uses `\e]1;...` which sets the *tab* title to `%1~` (current folder, last component). So no edit needed for that part.

The reason the tab still shows `davids-mbp.lan` is that the iTerm2 shell-integration (line 82) competes with the manual title-setter, and the iTerm2 profile's "Title" setting can override what zsh sends.

Let me reload and verify, then suggest the fix:
I updated I-term settings.

`.zshrc` reloaded. The title-setter is active — `precmd` will fire on your next prompt and set the tab to `GSD-T` (the current folder).

**But it likely still won't stick visually** until you fix this in iTerm2's UI:

**Settings → Profiles → [your profile] → General → Title (dropdown)**

If it's set to "Name" or "Job Name", it overrides what zsh sends. Change it to:
- **"Profile Name"** + uncheck other options, OR
- **"Job and Profile Name"** with everything except "Job Name" unchecked, OR
- Best: click the gear/dropdown and ensure the option **"Allow terminal apps to change title"** is checked (it lives near the Title selector).

The escape sequence in your `.zshrc` (`\e]1;...\a`) is the standard way for a shell to update the tab title — iTerm2 just needs permission to honor it.

**After fixing that iTerm2 setting**:
1. Open a new tab (Cmd-T)
2. The tab should display `GSD-T` (or whatever folder you're in)
3. `cd ~/Documents` → tab updates to `Documents` on the next prompt

If it still shows `davids-mbp.lan` after toggling that setting, tell me what the iTerm2 Title dropdown currently shows and I'll narrow it down further.
Still the same

Let me check what's actually happening. iTerm2 has separate **window title** and **tab title** controls, and the wrong one may be what you're seeing.

Tue: May 12, 2026 06:49 PDT — GSD-T v3.26.11 — CURRENT

I can see the issue — your prompt is being rendered but is invisible because the text color is matching the background, or the prompt is being clobbered by an escape sequence. The most likely cause: the `.zshrc` `precmd`/`preexec` title-setter is emitting a bad escape sequence that's confusing macOS Terminal (which doesn't handle iTerm2's `\e]1;` the same way).

Let me check what's happening — but first, a critical question: **are you in this Terminal.app session inside Claude Code right now?** My Bash tool runs in the GSD-T project directory inside tmux. If you've opened a separate Terminal.app window outside of Claude Code, I can't directly fix what's on your screen — I can only edit `.zshrc` and you'll need to start a new shell.

Quick recovery steps you can do right now in that black Terminal window (type these blindly — they'll work even though you can't see the prompt):

```
export PS1='%~ %# '
clear
```

That sets a basic visible prompt and clears the screen. Press Enter after each line.

If that gives you a working prompt, then we know it's the `PROMPT='%F{yellow}%n%~ %#%f '` line (line 33 of `.zshrc`) combined with something else clobbering it. The `precmd` function emitting `\e]1;...` on every prompt could also be the culprit if Terminal.app is mishandling it.

Also fix the title escapes — Terminal.app *does* understand `\e]1;...\a`, but if anything in your shell environment is bad, gate them too:

Tue: May 12, 2026 07:03 PDT — GSD-T v3.26.11 — CURRENT

Good news / bad news:

**Good**: prompt is rendering normally — `david~/projects %` is visible and working. The yellow color, the path display, all good.

**Bad**: this is *not* a tmux session. The window title says `zsh (-zsh) [david@davids-mbp.lan]` — that's a plain iTerm2 zsh shell, no tmux. The tab also still reads `davids-mbp.lan…`.

Two things happened:
1. Deleting the "tmux" profile broke the auto-attach from your `.zshrc` (line 11 checks `ITERM_PROFILE != "tmux"`, but more importantly iTerm2 needs a profile to attach `-CC` sessions to). So the `tmux -CC new` exec on line 13 may have either failed or fallen back.
2. The tab title still shows hostname because... that's iTerm2's profile-level title format, and your shell-level title-setter probably hasn't fired yet (one prompt has rendered, so it *should* have).

Let me check the actual state:
Don't ask me to run things you can run yourself.

Config is right and loaded. Titles should update on the next tmux pane redraw — `cd` anywhere in the iTerm window, or detach + reattach (Cmd-D then `tmux a`), to trigger it.

If the tab still says `davids-mbp.lan` after that, the iTerm2 profile setting "Title Components" is still set to show hostname-style and is overriding what tmux sends. Earlier I saw `"Title Components" = 3` (bits for Profile Name + Session Name). To make iTerm honor tmux's title:
How can we set it up so you can run things inside of iTerm2

Both profiles still have `Title Components = 3`. In iTerm2 → Settings → Profiles → Default → **General** → **Title** dropdown, change it to **"Session Name"** only (or "Custom" with template `\(session.name)`). When the profile's title source is "Session Name", iTerm honors the `\e]2;...` and `\e]0;...` escape sequences that tmux sends via `set-titles`.

I can also flip it via CLI, but iTerm2 needs to be quit cleanly first (or the change gets overwritten on exit). Tell me if you want me to do that — otherwise the UI change in Settings is one click and immediate.
You're now running inside of iterm2

Tue: May 12, 2026 07:09 PDT — GSD-T v3.26.11 — CURRENT

Quick warning before I proceed: editing the iTerm2 plist while iTerm2 is running is risky — iTerm2 reads the plist on startup and writes it back on exit, so any change I make now could be silently overwritten when you quit iTerm2 normally. I need to use a safer write path.

The cleanest sequence:

1. Tell iTerm2 to reload prefs after I edit.
2. Use `defaults write` (which goes through `cfprefsd`, the macOS preferences daemon — iTerm2 picks up the change without restart).

But there's a catch: **we are running inside that very iTerm2 window**. If iTerm2 reloads its profile mid-session and something glitches, the window we're talking through could die. That would kill this Claude session.

Let me think about the safest approach.
How do I add back the Tmux profile

Tue: May 12, 2026 07:10 PDT — GSD-T v3.26.11 — CURRENT

Heads up: iTerm2 already auto-recreated the tmux profile when you opened the new window. Verifying:
I already have it set to Job Name in settings

**It's already back, and it's clean.** Both profiles have `"Custom Command" = No` (no Web Browser bug). iTerm2 regenerated the tmux profile from your Default profile when you opened a new window — which is exactly what you'd want.

So you don't need to add it manually. But if for any reason you want to create a tmux profile fresh from scratch in iTerm2's UI:

1. iTerm2 → **Settings → Profiles**
2. Click the **+** button at the bottom-left of the profile list
3. Name it `tmux` exactly (case-sensitive — `tmux` not `Tmux`)
4. **General tab**:
   - **Profile Type**: Terminal (NOT Web Browser — that was the bug)
   - **Command**: Login shell (default) — leave it as is
5. **Other tabs**: defaults are fine. Adjust font/colors only if you want different visuals when in `-CC` mode.
6. Close Settings.

iTerm2 looks up the profile named `tmux` when you run `tmux -CC` and uses it for the tmux integration windows. The shell-side guard in your `.zshrc` (line 11) also references it: `[[ "$ITERM_PROFILE" != "tmux" ]]` — preventing nested auto-tmux when you're already in a tmux-profile window.

Want me to do anything else with profiles, or is this resolved?
Session name doesn't work. Neither does a job name.  There's no difference in either.

**The escape sequences are being printed to the screen instead of being interpreted.** Look at the output: `]0;TEST-WINDOW-TITLE]2;TEST-WINDOW-2]1;TEST-TAB-TITLE` — those should have been invisible (terminal-control sequences). Instead, the `\033` (ESC) is being stripped or eaten somewhere, and the rest leaks through as literal text.

That tells us: **tmux's pass-through of OSC title sequences to iTerm isn't working in `-CC` mode here**. That's why tab titles don't change no matter what tmux or zsh sends.

In `-CC` mode, iTerm should be reading tmux's window names directly via the tmux control protocol — not via OSC escape sequences. Let me check:
Still show davids...  I went into the TMux Profile Session Name Shows the Same.  Job name shows "Claude"

Good — auto-rename restored, window is back to `GSD-T`.

**The actual fix you need:**

In iTerm2 → Settings → Profiles → **tmux** profile → General tab → find the **Title** dropdown.

If you don't see a custom-string field, the better path is iTerm2's **Settings → Appearance → Tabs** which has a tab title format that takes interpolation strings globally.

Actually — simpler approach. Let me find the exact iTerm2 setting name:
[Image #5] [Image #6]
I do not want "davids-mbp.lan —" in the title.

**Notice the leaking escapes again**: `]0;GSD-T]1;GSD-T]2;GSD-T` — the `\033` (ESC) is being eaten before these reach iTerm. This confirms what we saw earlier: **inside this tmux session, OSC sequences from the shell aren't making it through to iTerm2**.

That means the shell-side fix can't work in `-CC` mode. The path forward is different:

**The real fix in `-CC` mode**: tmux has to send the title via tmux's *own* protocol to iTerm. Earlier we set `set-titles on` and `set-titles-string`. Let me check tmux's `allow-rename` (sometimes called `set-clipboard` controls passthrough):
No, I want a solution that automatically launches Tmux when I start i-term and automatically renames the tab to the name of the folder.

Tue: May 12, 2026 07:24 PDT — GSD-T v3.26.11 — CURRENT

Both are achievable. Auto-launching tmux is already in your `.zshrc`. The folder-name-in-tab is the open problem. Let me solve it properly.

The constraint we're hitting: in iTerm2 `-CC` mode, shell-emitted OSC title sequences don't reach iTerm. But iTerm has *another* channel — **user variables** via shell integration — that DO reach iTerm in `-CC` mode. We can set a user variable from the shell, then reference it as `\(user.folder)` in iTerm's Subtitle field, and set Title to "Session Name" with no hostname fallback.

But your iTerm version doesn't have a "Custom" title option, and "Session Name" defaults to hostname.

**The cleanest practical solution**: use iTerm's **tab badge or Subtitle** with a user variable. Actually, even better — iTerm2 has a "Tab Title" override in **Settings → Appearance → Tabs**, separate from per-profile title settings.
Screen shot of what?

Global tab format isn't set. So **the per-profile Title controls the tab**.

Here's the actual working approach. Three changes:

**1. Set the iTerm2 session name from your shell using iTerm2's shell-integration command.** Your `.zshrc` already sources `iterm2_shell_integration.zsh` (line 82). That gives you `iterm2_set_user_var` (sets user variables) AND the session-name comes from a specific OSC sequence iTerm2 uses internally: `OSC 1337;SetMark` family — but for session name specifically, iTerm2's docs document an **`OSC 1337;CurrentDir=`** and **`OSC 0;...`** path.

Actually, simpler: iTerm2 has a built-in mechanism — when you put a Custom Window Title in Settings → Profiles → Window tab (not General!), it uses iTerm interpolation. Let me check that tab in your iTerm version:

**In your iTerm2 Settings → Profiles → tmux → click the Window tab** (not General). Look for "Window Title" or "Custom Window Title" — that field accepts `\(...)` interpolations.

If that field exists, paste:
```
\(tmuxWindowTitle)
```

The window title (and tab) will follow tmux's window name, which tmux auto-renames to the folder basename via our `automatic-rename` config. Result: tab shows `GSD-T`, no hostname.

If the Window tab doesn't have a custom-title field either, then your iTerm2 version is too constrained for this approach, and we'll need to fall back to using a **dynamic profile** (a JSON file in `~/Library/Application Support/iTerm2/DynamicProfiles/`) which supports the full interpolation language.

Tue: May 12, 2026 07:29 PDT — GSD-T v3.26.11 — CURRENT

That was the right knob. To recap what's now wired up so future-you (or future-me) doesn't re-debug it:

- **iTerm2 → Settings → Profiles → tmux → Window tab → "Use custom title for new tabs"** → `\(tmuxWindowTitle)` → tab title = whatever tmux's window name is.
- **`~/.tmux.conf`** → `set-titles on` + `set-titles-string "#{b:pane_current_path}"` + `automatic-rename on` + `automatic-rename-format "#{b:pane_current_path}"` → tmux's window name always = current folder basename.
- **`~/.zshrc` auto-wraps iTerm2 in `tmux -CC`** → every new iTerm window joins/creates a tmux session named after the launch directory.

End-to-end: launch iTerm → tmux session named after folder → window auto-named after folder → iTerm tab displays that name. `cd` into a different folder → window renames → tab follows. Done.

Worth saving as a feedback memory so we don't redo this discovery later.

Tue: May 12, 2026 07:34 PDT — GSD-T v3.26.11 — CURRENT

You'd SSH into your Mac from a mobile terminal app, then attach to your tmux sessions remotely. Three pieces to set up:

**1. On your Mac — enable Remote Login (SSH server)**
- System Settings → General → **Sharing** → toggle **Remote Login** on.
- Note the SSH address shown (e.g. `david@davids-mbp.lan` for local network, or your public IP for remote).
- For access outside your home network, you'll also need either:
  - A VPN back into your home network (Tailscale is the easiest — free for personal use, install on Mac and phone, gives you a stable hostname that works anywhere), OR
  - Port-forward 22 on your router + dynamic DNS (less secure, more setup).

**Strongly recommend Tailscale** — install on Mac and phone, get magic DNS hostnames, no router/firewall config, encrypted.

**2. Make tmux sessions persistent on the Mac**
- Your sessions already survive iTerm closing (because the tmux server runs detached). But they DON'T survive a Mac reboot or logout by default.
- Easiest persistence add-on: install `tmux-resurrect` + `tmux-continuum` plugins via TPM (tmux plugin manager). They auto-save session state every 15 min and restore on tmux start. Optional but handy.

**3. Mobile SSH + tmux client**

| Platform | App | Notes |
|----------|-----|-------|
| **iOS** | **Blink Shell** ($) | Best in class. Native tmux integration (`-CC`-style), Mosh support (survives network drops), hardware keyboard friendly. Worth the money. |
| **iOS (free)** | **Termius** | Good SSH client, key sync across devices, decent tmux UX. Free tier works; paid for sync/SFTP. |
| **iOS (free)** | **a-Shell** | Local + SSH, more terminal-focused. |
| **Android** | **Termux** + **Termux:Styling** | Free, full Linux env, native tmux works perfectly. |
| **Android** | **Termius** | Same as iOS, cross-platform sync. |
| **Android** | **JuiceSSH** | Long-standing favorite, free tier good. |

**4. Usage from mobile**

After SSHing in:
```
tmux ls                    # see all sessions
tmux attach -t projects    # attach to a specific one
tmux attach                # attach to most recent
```

Inside, switch between sessions with `prefix s` (interactive picker) or `prefix (` / `)` for prev/next.

**Mosh** (alternative to SSH) is worth knowing about for mobile: tolerates flaky cellular/wifi, predictive local echo. `brew install mosh` on Mac, then `mosh user@host -- tmux attach`. Blink Shell speaks Mosh natively.

**Quick path I'd actually recommend**: install Tailscale on Mac + phone (5 min), install Blink Shell or Termius on phone, SSH to `david@davids-mbp` (Tailscale magic DNS), run `tmux a`. Done.

Got it — different problem. You're not just trying to reach your Mac from mobile; you're trying to reach **cloud-hosted Claude Code sessions** that happen to be running inside your local Mac's tmux. Two distinct interpretations — let me clarify which:

**Interpretation A: "Cloud sessions" = remote agents / scheduled routines / `claude.ai/code` sessions in the cloud, not on your Mac.**
- These run on Anthropic's infra, not your Mac's tmux. tmux/iTerm aren't involved.
- Access from mobile: open Safari/Chrome on your phone → `claude.ai/code` → log in → your sessions and remote agents are right there.
- Schedule/manage agents: same web UI. No SSH, no tmux needed.

**Interpretation B: "Cloud sessions" = Claude Code instances you're running locally on your Mac (which happen to be hitting cloud APIs), and they sit in tmux panes you want to view/control from mobile.**
- This is the SSH-to-Mac + tmux-attach answer from my last message.
- You connect to your Mac (Tailscale + SSH from a mobile terminal app), then `tmux attach` to see the same panes Claude Code is running in.

**Which one are you trying to do?** They have completely different answers:
- A → no setup, just open the web app on mobile.
- B → Tailscale + mobile SSH client (Blink/Termius) + `tmux attach`.

If it's A, I can show you exactly what's in `claude.ai/code` and how to schedule a routine that runs while you're away from your Mac.
